victim_of_misconduct.victim_of_misconduct_address_street_city: 7425 Sunrise Vista Drive, Apt 204, Citrus Heights, CA 95610

incident_details.location_of_incident: Greenback Lane & San Juan Avenue, Citrus Heights, CA 95621 (westbound curb lane, just east of the intersection)

witnesses.witness_address_row_1: 7401 Sunrise Vista Drive, Apt 210, Citrus Heights, CA 95610

description_of_events.description_of_events_line_1: On Saturday night, 2025-11-29, at approximately 9:15 PM, I (Daniel Alejandro Ramirez) was driving westbound on Greenback Lane approaching San Juan Avenue in Citrus Heights after finishing a late shift at my job in downtown Sacramento. I was in my silver 2014 Honda Civic (CA plate 7FHY291), in the right-hand lane, and had just passed the Walmart shopping center. I was not speeding (I had my cruise control set at about 40 mph in the posted 40 mph zone), and I had not been drinking or using any drugs. I was on my way home to my apartment on Sunrise Vista Drive, talking to my neighbor and friend, Melissa Nguyen, on my hands-free Bluetooth system about plans for the next day. As I approached the intersection, I saw emergency lights behind me and heard a quick burst of a siren. I immediately signaled and safely pulled over to the curb close to the bus stop just before the intersection.
description_of_events.description_of_events_line_2: A Citrus Heights Police Department patrol vehicle (later identified as Unit #214) pulled in behind me with its spotlight pointed directly into my side mirror, making it hard to see. After about a minute, a white male officer in uniform, later identifying himself as Officer Michael J. Turner (Badge #137), approached my driver-side window. Before I could even fully roll the window down, he was already shouting commands in a very aggressive tone, saying things like, "Keep your hands where I can see them!" and "Don’t move!" I was already resting both hands on top of the steering wheel where they were plainly visible. I calmly responded, "My hands are here on the wheel, officer," but he continued to shout. He did not initially tell me why I was being stopped. When I tried to ask, "Can you please tell me what this is about?" he cut me off and said, "I’ll ask the questions. You answer. Do you understand?" in a very hostile and demeaning way. I felt intimidated and confused because I had not done anything wrong and was fully cooperating.
description_of_events.description_of_events_line_3: Officer Turner then demanded my license, registration, and proof of insurance. I informed him that my wallet was in my right back pocket and my registration and insurance card were in the glove compartment, and I asked for permission to reach for them, keeping my hands visible. Instead of acknowledging this reasonably, he rolled his eyes and said in a sarcastic voice, "Yeah, I’ve heard that before," implying I might be lying or trying something. He then yanked my door open without warning, grabbed my left forearm hard enough to leave red marks, and pulled me partially out of the car even though I was wearing my seatbelt and had not made any sudden movements. When I said, "You’re hurting me, I’m not resisting," he replied, "Then stop acting suspicious." At this point my phone, still connected to Bluetooth, had the call with Melissa ongoing; she heard him yelling at me and me saying that he was hurting my arm. After about a minute of this, another officer arrived on scene (I don’t know his name), and only then did Officer Turner finally state that he had stopped me because he claimed I had been "weaving" and "might be DUI." I explained that I had not been drinking at all and offered to do any tests necessary. Instead of conducting a standard, calm DUI investigation, he continued to speak to me in a disrespectful and accusatory manner, making comments like, "People like you always say you haven’t been drinking" and "If you don’t want trouble, you should drive better," which I perceived as biased and unprofessional. Ultimately, after having me stand on the sidewalk in full view of passing traffic for nearly 25 minutes and performing field sobriety tests that I passed, he released me without a citation and said, "You’re lucky I’m letting you go," without any acknowledgment of his conduct. I am filing this complaint because I believe Officer Turner used unnecessary physical force when grabbing and pulling me from my vehicle, failed to clearly explain the reason for the stop in a timely and respectful manner, and treated me in a degrading, intimidating way despite my full cooperation. This incident has left me feeling anxious whenever I see police vehicles, and my right forearm was sore and visibly red for the rest of the night; my neighbor Melissa saw the marks shortly after I got home and can verify what I told her on the phone and what she observed in person.
